@1: Computer Science IV: Independent Studies is a class designated to be the last class a student studying computer science would take. As a result, the class is a lot looser than its prerequisites. Contrary to its name, the class is actually a series of labs and projects centered around the programming language chosen by the teacher. Such language usually changed every couple of months or so, based on the whims of the teacher. As a result, we were forced to experiment with new programming techniques and ways of thinking, as the same old solutions we knew were no longer viable to solve our new problems.
@2: The first language we learned in the class was python. We started out with the basics of language but then quickly progressed into UI based games. They were small games aimed at being completed in around a week each; in other words, they were simple games with simple graphics.
@3: The first game we made was Tic-Tac-Toe. Made from following a youtube tutorial step by step, the final product is unimpressive, inefficient, and a carbon copy of every other Tic-Tac-Toe game in the class.
@4: After that, we made Connect 4. Using the knowledge gained from Tic-Tac-Toe (and no longer bound by the constraints of a tutorial), we were allowed to create our own rendition of Connect 4 however we wanted to. However, I didn't stray too far from the formula -- at least visually. Under the surface, there were many performance optimizations which I was able to implement, which resulted in a basic looking but quick-paced game.
@5: After about two weeks of that introduction to the language, we moved into the common uses of python: data structures. Most notably, we worked with JSON, CSV, and XML files. By the end of the unit, once we were familiar with all of the file types, their advantages, disadvantages, and intricacies, we moved onto our final python project related to data structures involving SQL.
